pr.title: ft: Match ftKb_SpecialN_800F11AC and fn_800F1CA0 in ftKb_Init.c
pr.author: itsgrimetime

summary: Closed, unmerged PR that attempted to match two Kirby init/SpecialN functions in src/melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/ftKb_Init.c and flip ftKb_Init.c from NonMatching to Matching in configure.py. The submitted implementations replaced two placeholder decomp markers with C code for ftKb_SpecialN_800F11AC and fn_800F1CA0, but the PR was closed because the worktree commits had build issues that needed fixing before batching.
what_changed: Added a C implementation for ftKb_SpecialN_800F11AC in ftKb_Init.c. The function obtains Fighter* from gobj->user_data, calls ftKb_SpecialN_800EF69C(gobj, 0xF, ft_80459B88.hats[14]), then calls ftCo_UnloadDynamicBones(fp).

Added a C implementation for fn_800F1CA0 in ftKb_Init.c. The function obtains Fighter* from gobj->user_data, reads a u32 at Fighter offset 0x2238 via M2C_FIELD(fp, u32*, 0x2238), and returns true for values 2, 7, 10, 11, or 13, false otherwise.
Changed configure.py so melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/ftKb_Init.c was built as Object(Matching, ...) instead of Object(NonMatching, ...).
The PR body claimed both target functions matched 100%, but the PR was closed without merge due to build issues in the worktree commits.
smart_moves: For ftKb_SpecialN_800F11AC, the implementation reused an existing helper, ftKb_SpecialN_800EF69C, and an existing global hat table entry, ft_80459B88.hats[14], instead of open-coding Kirby hat setup behavior.
The dynamic-bone cleanup was explicit: after the Kirby SpecialN helper call, the implementation invoked ftCo_UnloadDynamicBones on the Fighter pointer.

For fn_800F1CA0, the boolean condition was expressed as a switch over exact integer cases, a clear structure for matching branch-table or compare-chain behavior when the original only accepts a sparse set of action/state values.
decomp_lessons: Do not flip an entire source file from NonMatching to Matching in configure.py unless the full translation unit builds and matches in CI; this PR was closed specifically because build issues remained.

When matching small fighter helper predicates, preserving exact sparse value sets can be more important than giving the field a guessed semantic name. This PR used M2C_FIELD at offset 0x2238, which is evidence that the field was still unidentified.
Helper reuse can make short Kirby init/SpecialN functions compact: ftKb_SpecialN_800F11AC was reduced to user_data extraction, an existing Kirby SpecialN helper call, and dynamic-bone unload.
A local 100% match claim for individual functions is not sufficient for acceptance if the worktree or batch has build failures.
naming_conventions: Existing decomp placeholder naming was retained for unknown or not-yet-renamed functions: ftKb_SpecialN_800F11AC and fn_800F1CA0.
Kirby fighter code lives under src/melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/ and uses the ftKb_ prefix.
SpecialN-related Kirby routines in this file follow names like ftKb_SpecialN_<address> when a more descriptive name is not yet established.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used M2C_FIELD(fp, u32*, 0x2238) for an unidentified Fighter field rather than inventing a struct member name.
Modeled a sparse accepted-value predicate with a switch containing fallthrough cases 2, 7, 10, 11, and 13 returning true, with default returning false.

Matched a short side-effect function by preserving call order: read Fighter from gobj->user_data, call ftKb_SpecialN_800EF69C with immediate 0xF and ft_80459B88.hats[14], then call ftCo_UnloadDynamicBones.

review_feedback.feedback: Closing - worktree commits have build issues that need to be fixed at the source before batching.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that match ftKb_SpecialN_800F11AC or fn_800F1CA0 after fixing the build issues.
Search for the final typed name or struct field replacing M2C_FIELD(fp, u32*, 0x2238) in Kirby/Fighter code.
Check whether ftKb_Init.c was eventually flipped to Object(Matching) in configure.py and which functions remained blocking.
Investigate the exact build errors from the worktree commits if logs are available.
